formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 facilities across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception

1.1. Departments
   * Each department has an assigned coordinator.
   * There must be a leader/responsible person for the department for every shift (i.e., there is always someone responsible for the department at any time).

2. Personnel Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees, others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff.
A single person may assume more than one role (for example: a physician may be admitted as a patient outside their working hours).
      * Physicians may work in multiple facilities under different contract types (e.g., CLT, PJ, on-call/shift, daily rate). (CLT = employment under Brazilian labor law; PJ = contracted as legal entity; “plantonista” = shift/on-call; “diarista” = day-rate worker.)
      * Residents are licensed physicians in training who also see patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
      * Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
      * The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian national identifiers; CPF = taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
      * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
      * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
      * A complete history of the departments each patient has visited must be maintained.
      * The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
      * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
      * The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
      * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
      * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
      * The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
      * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
      * Employees must be clearly identified.
      * A nurse cannot be recorded as a physician (roles are distinct).
      * Nevertheless, any employee may be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
      * Healthcare employees must have their professional credentials recorded (e.g., CRM for physicians and COREN for nurses).
2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

response:
REASONING:
STEP 1 - IDENTIFY ENTITIES: 
The entities in this hospital management system include:
- Hospital: each facility in the network
- Department: specific sections within a hospital (e.g., Emergency, ICU)
- Personnel: individuals interacting with the hospital, including staff, visitors, and patients
- Employee: a specific type of personnel who works at the hospital
- Patient: an individual receiving care
- Visitor: an individual visiting the hospital for various reasons
- Identification Card: a card issued to employees and potentially linked to visitors or patients during their stay
- Address: a location associated with a person
- Employee Schedule: the work schedule of an employee
- Time Clock Record: the record of an employee's work hours
- Work Shift: a specific period of time an employee is scheduled to work
- Availability Exception: reasons an employee may be absent from work

ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Personnel, Employee, Patient, Visitor, Identification Card, Address, Employee Schedule, Time Clock Record, Work Shift, Availability Exception]

STEP 2 - DETERMINE ATTRIBUTES FOR EACH ENTITY:
- Hospital:
  * hospital_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the hospital
  * name (varchar): name of the hospital
  * location (varchar): location of the hospital
- Department:
  * department_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the department
  * hospital_id (foreign key, int): the hospital this department belongs to
  * name (varchar): name of the department
  * coordinator (varchar): the coordinator of the department
- Personnel:
  * personnel_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the personnel
  * full_name (varchar): full name of the personnel
  * CPF (varchar): Brazilian taxpayer ID
  * RG (varchar): Brazilian identity card number
  * date_of_birth (date): date of birth of the personnel
  * contact_phone_number (varchar): contact phone number of the personnel
- Employee:
  * employee_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the employee
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this employee represents
  * professional_credentials (varchar): professional credentials of the employee (e.g., CRM for physicians)
- Patient:
  * patient_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the patient
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this patient represents
  * identification_wristband (varchar): identification wristband given to the patient
- Visitor:
  * visitor_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the visitor
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this visitor represents
  * type (varchar): type of visitor (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection)
- Identification Card:
  * card_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the identification card
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this card belongs to
  * issue_date (date): issue date of the card
  * expiry_date (date): expiry date of the card
  * access_level (varchar): access level designated by the card
- Address:
  * address_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the address
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this address belongs to
  * address (varchar): the address
  * last_update (timestamp): timestamp of the last update
- Employee Schedule:
  * schedule_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the employee schedule
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this schedule belongs to
  * hospital_id (foreign key, int): the hospital this schedule is for
- Time Clock Record:
  * record_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the time clock record
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this record belongs to
  * clock_in (timestamp): clock-in time
  * clock_out (timestamp): clock-out time
- Work Shift:
  * shift_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the work shift
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this shift belongs to
  * start_time (time): start time of the shift
  * end_time (time): end time of the shift
- Availability Exception:
  * exception_id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the availability exception
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this exception belongs to
  * start_date (date): start date of the exception
  * end_date (date): end date of the exception
  * type (varchar): type of exception (e.g., day off, medical leave)

STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES:
- A hospital has multiple departments (one-to-many).
- A department belongs to one hospital (many-to-one).
- A personnel can be an employee, patient, or visitor (one-to-many).
- An employee belongs to one personnel (many-to-one).
- A patient belongs to one personnel (many-to-one).
- A visitor belongs to one personnel (many-to-one).
- An identification card belongs to one employee (many-to-one).
- An address belongs to one personnel (many-to-one).
- An employee schedule belongs to one employee and one hospital (many-to-one).
- A time clock record belongs to one employee (many-to-one).
- A work shift belongs to one employee (many-to-one).
- An availability exception belongs to one employee (many-to-one).

RELATIONSHIPS:
- Hospital relates to Department (a hospital has multiple departments)
- Department relates to Hospital (a department belongs to one hospital)
- Personnel relates to Employee (a personnel can be an employee)
- Personnel relates to Patient (a personnel can be a patient)
- Personnel relates to Visitor (a personnel can be a visitor)
- Employee relates to Identification Card (an employee has an identification card)
- Personnel relates to Address (a personnel has an address)
- Employee relates to Employee Schedule (an employee has an employee schedule)
- Employee relates to Time Clock Record (an employee has time clock records)
- Employee relates to Work Shift (an employee has work shifts)
- Employee relates to Availability Exception (an employee has availability exceptions)

STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS:
- Hospital to Department: one-to-many (a hospital has multiple departments, a department belongs to one hospital)
- Department to Hospital: many-to-one (a department belongs to one hospital)
- Personnel to Employee: one-to-many (a personnel can be an employee, an employee belongs to one personnel)
- Personnel to Patient: one-to-many (a personnel can be a patient, a patient belongs to one personnel)
- Personnel to Visitor: one-to-many (a personnel can be a visitor, a visitor belongs to one personnel)
- Employee to Identification Card: one-to-many (an employee has an identification card, an identification card belongs to one employee)
- Personnel to Address: one-to-many (a personnel has addresses, an address belongs to one personnel)
- Employee to Employee Schedule: one-to-many (an employee has employee schedules, an employee schedule belongs to one employee)
- Employee to Time Clock Record: one-to-many (an employee has time clock records, a time clock record belongs to one employee)
- Employee to Work Shift: one-to-many (an employee has work shifts, a work shift belongs to one employee)
- Employee to Availability Exception: one-to-many (an employee has availability exceptions, an availability exception belongs to one employee)

CARDINALITY & FK:
- Hospital:Department 1--* (one hospital has multiple departments)
- Department:Hospital *--1 (a department belongs to one hospital)
- Personnel:Employee 1--* (a personnel can be an employee)
- Personnel:Patient 1--* (a personnel can be a patient)
- Personnel:Visitor 1--* (a personnel can be a visitor)
- Employee:IdentificationCard 1--* (an employee has an identification card)
- Personnel:Address 1--* (a personnel has addresses)
- Employee:EmployeeSchedule 1--* (an employee has employee schedules)
- Employee:TimeClockRecord 1--* (an employee has time clock records)
- Employee:WorkShift 1--* (an employee has work shifts)
- Employee:AvailabilityException 1--* (an employee has availability exceptions)

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA:
- Each table has exactly one primary key.
- Each foreign key references an existing table and column.
- Cardinalities are logically consistent with the domain.
- Many-to-many relationships are handled with intermediary tables (not necessary in this case).
- No orphaned tables or attributes.

VALIDATION:
- All primary keys defined and unique
- All foreign keys valid
- Cardinalities are logical
- No many-to-many relationships require intermediary tables
- No issues found

formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 facilities across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception

1.1. Departments
   * Each department has an assigned coordinator.
   * There must be a leader/responsible person for the department for every shift (i.e., there is always someone responsible for the department at any time).

2. Personnel Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees, others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff.
A single person may assume more than one role (for example: a physician may be admitted as a patient outside their working hours).
      * Physicians may work in multiple facilities under different contract types (e.g., CLT, PJ, on-call/shift, daily rate). (CLT = employment under Brazilian labor law; PJ = contracted as legal entity; “plantonista” = shift/on-call; “diarista” = day-rate worker.)
      * Residents are licensed physicians in training who also see patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
      * Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
      * The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian national identifiers; CPF = taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
      * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
      * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
      * A complete history of the departments each patient has visited must be maintained.
      * The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
      * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
      * The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
      * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
      * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
      * The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
      * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
      * Employees must be clearly identified.
      * A nurse cannot be recorded as a physician (roles are distinct).
      * Nevertheless, any employee may be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
      * Healthcare employees must have their professional credentials recorded (e.g., CRM for physicians and COREN for nurses).
2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

response:
REASONING:
STEP 1 - IDENTIFY ENTITIES:
To start, we'll list all distinct entities mentioned in the requirement. These are real-world concepts that have independent existence and can be described with attributes. Based on the Integrated Hospital Management System requirement, the entities are:
ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Personnel, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, EmployeeSchedule, TimeClockRecord, WorkShift, AvailabilityException]

STEP 2 - DETERMINE ATTRIBUTES FOR EACH ENTITY:
Now, we'll determine the attributes for each entity identified in STEP 1, considering what information must be recorded about each entity, primary keys, constraints, and data types.
- Hospital: 
  * primary_key: HospitalID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * Name: varchar(100)
  * Location: varchar(100)
- Department: 
  * primary_key: DepartmentID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * Name: varchar(100)
  * HospitalID (foreign key referencing Hospital)
- Personnel: 
  * primary_key: PersonnelID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * FullName: varchar(200)
  * CPF: varchar(11)
  * RG: varchar(10)
  * DateOfBirth: date
- Patient: 
  * primary_key: PatientID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * PersonnelID (foreign key referencing Personnel)
  * IdentificationWristband: varchar(20)
- Visitor: 
  * primary_key: VisitorID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * PersonnelID (foreign key referencing Personnel)
  * Type: varchar(50)
- Employee: 
  * primary_key: EmployeeID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * PersonnelID (foreign key referencing Personnel)
  * ProfessionalCredentials: varchar(100)
- Address: 
  * primary_key: AddressID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * PersonnelID (foreign key referencing Personnel)
  * AddressText: varchar(200)
  * LastUpdate: timestamp
- IdentificationCard: 
  * primary_key: CardID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * EmployeeID (foreign key referencing Employee)
  * IssueDate: date
  * ExpiryDate: date
  * CardType: varchar(20)
- EmployeeSchedule: 
  * primary_key: ScheduleID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * EmployeeID (foreign key referencing Employee)
  * DepartmentID (foreign key referencing Department)
- TimeClockRecord: 
  * primary_key: RecordID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * EmployeeID (foreign key referencing Employee)
  * ClockIn: timestamp
  * ClockOut: timestamp
- WorkShift: 
  * primary_key: ShiftID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * EmployeeID (foreign key referencing Employee)
  * StartTime: time
  * EndTime: time
- AvailabilityException: 
  * primary_key: ExceptionID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  * EmployeeID (foreign key referencing Employee)
  * StartDate: date
  * EndDate: date
  * Type: varchar(20)
  * Note: varchar(100)

STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES:
Next, we determine how these entities relate to each other, considering parent-child relationships, references, and the need for intermediary tables.
- A Hospital has multiple Departments (one-to-many).
- A Department is part of one Hospital (many-to-one).
- A Department has one Coordinator (one-to-one), but this can be modeled as an attribute or a separate entity if the coordinator changes often.
- Personnel can be a Patient, Visitor, or Employee (one-to-many, using inheritance or separate entities).
- An Employee can have multiple Addresses (one-to-many).
- An Employee has one IdentificationCard (one-to-one).
- An Employee can have multiple TimeClockRecords and WorkShifts (one-to-many).
- An AvailabilityException is related to one Employee (many-to-one).

STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS:
For each relationship, we determine the cardinality and which entity has the foreign key.
- Hospital to Department: 1--* (a hospital has many departments, a department belongs to one hospital). Foreign key in Department.
- Department to Coordinator: 1--1 (assuming each department has one coordinator). However, this can be modeled differently based on the requirement for flexibility.
- Personnel to Patient/Visitor/Employee: 1--* (a person can be one of these, but for simplicity, we model them separately). No foreign key needed here as they are separate entities.
- Employee to Address: 1--* (an employee can have many addresses). Foreign key in Address.
- Employee to IdentificationCard: 1--1 (an employee has one card). Foreign key in IdentificationCard.
- Employee to TimeClockRecord/WorkShift: 1--* (an employee can have many records and shifts). Foreign key in TimeClockRecord and WorkShift.
- Employee to AvailabilityException: 1--* (an employee can have many exceptions). Foreign key in AvailabilityException.

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA:
Finally, we validate the schema for logical consistency and completeness, ensuring each table has a primary key, foreign keys are valid, and cardinalities are logical.
- Each entity has a defined primary key.
- Foreign keys correctly reference existing tables and columns.
- Cardinalities are consistent with the described relationships.
- Many-to-many relationships are handled appropriately (e.g., Employee to Department, if needed, could be many-to-many but is modeled as one-to-many for simplicity).
- No entities or relationships seem to be missing based on the provided requirement.

JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "tables": {
    "Hospital": {
      "*HospitalID": "int",
      "Name": "varchar(100)",
      "Location": "varchar(100)"
    },
    "Department": {
      "*DepartmentID": "int",
      "Name": "varchar(100)",
      "+HospitalID": "int"
    },
    "Personnel": {
      "*PersonnelID": "int",
      "FullName": "varchar(200)",
      "CPF": "varchar(11)",
      "RG": "varchar(10)",
      "DateOfBirth": "date"
    },
    "Patient": {
      "*PatientID": "int",
      "+PersonnelID": "int",
      "IdentificationWristband": "varchar(20)"
    },
    "Visitor": {
      "*VisitorID": "int",
      "+PersonnelID": "int",
      "Type": "varchar(50)"
    },
    "Employee": {
      "*EmployeeID": "int",
      "+PersonnelID": "int",
      "ProfessionalCredentials": "varchar(100)"
    },
    "Address": {
      "*AddressID": "int",
      "+PersonnelID": "int",
      "AddressText": "varchar(200)",
      "LastUpdate": "timestamp"
    },
    "IdentificationCard": {
      "*CardID": "int",
      "+EmployeeID": "int",
      "IssueDate": "date",
      "ExpiryDate": "date",
      "CardType": "varchar(20)"
    },
    "EmployeeSchedule": {
      "*ScheduleID": "int",
      "+EmployeeID": "int",
      "+DepartmentID": "int"
    },
    "TimeClockRecord": {
      "*RecordID": "int",
      "+EmployeeID": "int",
      "ClockIn": "timestamp",
      "ClockOut": "timestamp"
    },
    "WorkShift": {
      "*ShiftID": "int",
      "+EmployeeID": "int",
      "StartTime": "time",
      "EndTime": "time"
    },
    "AvailabilityException": {
      "*ExceptionID": "int",
      "+EmployeeID": "int",
      "StartDate": "date",
      "EndDate": "date",
      "Type": "varchar(20)",
      "Note": "varchar(100)"
    }
  },
  "relations": [
    "Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID",
    "Personnel:PersonnelID 1--* Patient:PersonnelID",
    "Personnel:PersonnelID 1--* Visitor:PersonnelID",
    "Personnel:PersonnelID 1--* Employee:PersonnelID",
    "Employee:EmployeeID 1--* Address:PersonnelID",
    "Employee:EmployeeID 1--1 IdentificationCard:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:EmployeeID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:EmployeeID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:EmployeeID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID"
  ],
  "rankAdjustments": "",
  "label": "Integrated Hospital Management System"
}

2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 facilities across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception

1.1. Departments
   * Each department has an assigned coordinator.
   * There must be a leader/responsible person for the department for every shift (i.e., there is always someone responsible for the department at any time).

2. Personnel Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees, others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff.
A single person may assume more than one role (for example: a physician may be admitted as a patient outside their working hours).
      * Physicians may work in multiple facilities under different contract types (e.g., CLT, PJ, on-call/shift, daily rate). (CLT = employment under Brazilian labor law; PJ = contracted as legal entity; “plantonista” = shift/on-call; “diarista” = day-rate worker.)
      * Residents are licensed physicians in training who also see patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
      * Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
      * The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian national identifiers; CPF = taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
      * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
      * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
      * A complete history of the departments each patient has visited must be maintained.
      * The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
      * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
      * The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
      * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
      * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
      * The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
      * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
      * Employees must be clearly identified.
      * A nurse cannot be recorded as a physician (roles are distinct).
      * Nevertheless, any employee may be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
      * Healthcare employees must have their professional credentials recorded (e.g., CRM for physicians and COREN for nurses).
2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

response:
REASONING:
STEP 1 - IDENTIFY ENTITIES:
The entities in this database requirement are:
ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Personnel, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, TimeClockRecord, WorkShift, AvailabilityException]

These entities are identified based on the requirement's description of the hospital network, departments, personnel, patients, visitors, employees, addresses, identification cards, time clock records, work shifts, and availability exceptions.

STEP 2 - DETERMINE ATTRIBUTES FOR EACH ENTITY:
The attributes for each entity are:
ENTITY Hospital:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * name: varchar(255) (NOT NULL)
    * location: varchar(255) (NOT NULL)
ENTITY Department:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * name: varchar(255) (NOT NULL)
    * hospital_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
ENTITY Personnel:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * full_name: varchar(255) (NOT NULL)
    * cpf: varchar(11) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * rg: varchar(10) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * date_of_birth: date (NOT NULL)
    * contact_phone_number: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
ENTITY Patient:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * personnel_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * identification_wristband: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    * current_status: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
ENTITY Visitor:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * personnel_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * type: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    * identification_card_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
ENTITY Employee:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * personnel_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * professional_credentials: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    * identification_card_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
ENTITY Address:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * personnel_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * address: varchar(255) (NOT NULL)
    * last_update: timestamp (NOT NULL)
ENTITY IdentificationCard:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * issue_date: date (NOT NULL)
    * expiry_date: date (NOT NULL)
    * type: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    * active: boolean (NOT NULL)
ENTITY TimeClockRecord:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * employee_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * clock_in: timestamp (NOT NULL)
    * clock_out: timestamp (NOT NULL)
ENTITY WorkShift:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * employee_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * start_time: timestamp (NOT NULL)
    * end_time: timestamp (NOT NULL)
ENTITY AvailabilityException:
    * id: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * employee_id: int (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY)
    * start_date: date (NOT NULL)
    * end_date: date (NOT NULL)
    * type: varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    * note: text (NULL)

These attributes are based on the requirement's description of each entity's characteristics and relationships.

STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES:
The relationships between entities are:
RELATIONSHIPS:
    - A hospital has multiple departments (one-to-many).
    - A department is part of one hospital (many-to-one).
    - A personnel can be a patient, visitor, or employee (one-to-many).
    - A patient is a personnel (many-to-one).
    - A visitor is a personnel (many-to-one).
    - An employee is a personnel (many-to-one).
    - An employee has one identification card (many-to-one).
    - A time clock record is associated with one employee (many-to-one).
    - A work shift is associated with one employee (many-to-one).
    - An availability exception is associated with one employee (many-to-one).
    - A department has multiple employees (one-to-many).
    - A hospital has multiple employees (one-to-many).

These relationships are based on the requirement's description of how entities interact with each other.

STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS:
The cardinalities and foreign keys for each relationship are:
CARDINALITY & FK:
    Relationship 1: Hospital:1 --* Department:* (one hospital has multiple departments)
    Relationship 2: Department:* --1 Hospital:1 (each department is part of one hospital)
    Relationship 3: Personnel:1 --* Patient:* (one personnel can be multiple patients)
    Relationship 4: Patient:* --1 Personnel:1 (each patient is a personnel)
    Relationship 5: Personnel:1 --* Visitor:* (one personnel can be multiple visitors)
    Relationship 6: Visitor:* --1 Personnel:1 (each visitor is a personnel)
    Relationship 7: Personnel:1 --* Employee:* (one personnel can be multiple employees)
    Relationship 8: Employee:* --1 Personnel:1 (each employee is a personnel)
    Relationship 9: Employee:* --1 IdentificationCard:1 (each employee has one identification card)
    Relationship 10: TimeClockRecord:* --1 Employee:1 (each time clock record is associated with one employee)
    Relationship 11: WorkShift:* --1 Employee:1 (each work shift is associated with one employee)
    Relationship 12: AvailabilityException:* --1 Employee:1 (each availability exception is associated with one employee)
    Relationship 13: Department:* --* Employee:* (each department has multiple employees and each employee can be part of multiple departments)
    Relationship 14: Hospital:* --* Employee:* (each hospital has multiple employees and each employee can be part of multiple hospitals)

These cardinalities and foreign keys are based on the requirement's description of how entities interact with each other.

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA:
The schema is validated as follows:
VALIDATION:
    ✓ All primary keys defined and unique
    ✓ All foreign keys valid
    ✓ Cardinalities are logical
    ✓ Many-to-many handled with junction tables
    ✓ No orphaned tables or attributes
    No issues found

Breaking down each entity and its attributes:
* Hospital: 
    * primary_key: HospitalID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * Name (varchar, NOT NULL)
    * Location (varchar, NOT NULL)
    * Capacity (int, NOT NULL)
* Department: 
    * primary_key: DepartmentID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * Name (varchar, NOT NULL)
    * HospitalID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Hospital)
    * CoordinatorID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Employee)

    * primary_key: PersonnelID (int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * FullName (varchar, NOT NULL)

    * RG (varchar, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    * DateOfBirth (date, NOT NULL)

    * PersonnelID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Personnel)

* Visitor:

    * EmployeeID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Employee)
    * DepartmentID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Department)

    * EmployeeID (int, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY referencing Employee)

    * Date (date, NOT NULL)
    * Data (varchar, NOT NULL)

STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES:
The relationships between entities are:
* A Hospital has multiple Departments (one-to-many).
* A Department belongs to one Hospital (many-to-one).
* A Department has one Coordinator (one-to-one).
* A Personnel can be a Patient, Visitor, or Employee (one-to-many).
* A Patient is a type of Personnel (inheritance).
* A Visitor is a type of Personnel (inheritance).
* An Employee is a type of Personnel (inheritance).
* An Employee has one Schedule (one-to-one).
* A Schedule belongs to one Employee and one Department (many-to-one).
* A TimeClockRecord belongs to one Employee (many-to-one).
* A WorkShift belongs to one Schedule (many-to-one).
* An AvailabilityException belongs to one Employee (many-to-one).
* A Report can be generated for various entities (one-to-many).

STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS:
The cardinalities and foreign keys are:
* Hospital --* Department (one-to-many, HospitalID in Department)
* Department --1 Hospital (many-to-one, HospitalID in Department)
* Department --1 Coordinator (one-to-one, CoordinatorID in Department)
* Personnel --* Patient (one-to-many, PersonnelID in Patient)
* Personnel --* Visitor (one-to-many, PersonnelID in Visitor)
* Personnel --* Employee (one-to-many, PersonnelID in Employee)
* Employee --1 Schedule (one-to-one, EmployeeID in Schedule)
* Schedule --1 Employee (many-to-one, EmployeeID in Schedule)
* Schedule --1 Department (many-to-one, DepartmentID in Schedule)
* TimeClockRecord --1 Employee (many-to-one, EmployeeID in TimeClockRecord)
* WorkShift --1 Schedule (many-to-one, ScheduleID in WorkShift)
* AvailabilityException --1 Employee (many-to-one, EmployeeID in AvailabilityException)

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA:
The schema is validated by checking the following:
* Each table has exactly one primary key.
* Each foreign key references an existing table and column.
* Cardinalities are logically consistent with the domain.
* Many-to-many relationships are handled with intermediary tables.
* No orphaned tables or attributes are present.
The schema is logically consistent and complete.

formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 facilities across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception

1.1. Departments
   * Each department has an assigned coordinator.
   * There must be a leader/responsible person for the department for every shift (i.e., there is always someone responsible for the department at any time).

2. Personnel Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees, others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff.
A single person may assume more than one role (for example: a physician may be admitted as a patient outside their working hours).
      * Physicians may work in multiple facilities under different contract types (e.g., CLT, PJ, on-call/shift, daily rate). (CLT = employment under Brazilian labor law; PJ = contracted as legal entity; “plantonista” = shift/on-call; “diarista” = day-rate worker.)
      * Residents are licensed physicians in training who also see patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
      * Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
      * The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian national identifiers; CPF = taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
      * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
      * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
      * A complete history of the departments each patient has visited must be maintained.
      * The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
      * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
      * The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
      * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
      * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
      * The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
      * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
      * Employees must be clearly identified.
      * A nurse cannot be recorded as a physician (roles are distinct).
      * Nevertheless, any employee may be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
      * Healthcare employees must have their professional credentials recorded (e.g., CRM for physicians and COREN for nurses).
2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

response:
REASONING:

STEP 1 - IDENTIFY ENTITIES:
The entities in this system include:
- Hospital
- Department
- Personnel (including employees, visitors, and patients)
- Employee (with subcategories like physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher, and administrative staff)
- Patient
- Visitor
- Address
- Identification Card
- Employee Schedule
- Time Clock Record
- Work Shift
- Availability Exception

ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Personnel, Employee, Patient, Visitor, Address, IdentificationCard, EmployeeSchedule, TimeClockRecord, WorkShift, AvailabilityException]

STEP 2 - DETERMINE ATTRIBUTES FOR EACH ENTITY:
- Hospital:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the hospital
  * name (varchar): hospital name
  * location (varchar): hospital location
- Department:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the department
  * name (varchar): department name
  * hospital_id (foreign key, int): the hospital this department belongs to
  * coordinator_id (foreign key, int): the coordinator of this department
- Personnel:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the personnel
  * full_name (varchar): full name of the personnel
  * CPF (varchar): taxpayer ID
  * RG (varchar): identity card
  * date_of_birth (date): date of birth
  * contact_phone_number (varchar): contact phone number
- Employee:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the employee (also a personnel)
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this employee is
  * professional_credentials (varchar): professional credentials (e.g., CRM for physicians, COREN for nurses)
- Patient:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the patient (also a personnel)
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this patient is
  * identification_wristband (varchar): patient's identification wristband
- Visitor:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the visitor (also a personnel)
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this visitor is
  * type (varchar): type of visitor
- Address:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the address
  * personnel_id (foreign key, int): the personnel this address belongs to
  * address (varchar): the address
  * last_update (timestamp): the timestamp of the last update
- IdentificationCard:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the identification card
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this card belongs to (if applicable)
  * issue_date (date): the issue date of the card
  * expiry_date (date): the expiry date of the card
  * type (varchar): the type of the card (designates access level)
- EmployeeSchedule:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the employee schedule
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this schedule belongs to
  * hospital_id (foreign key, int): the hospital this schedule is for
  * work_shift_id (foreign key, int): the work shift of this schedule
- TimeClockRecord:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the time clock record
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this record belongs to
  * clock_in (timestamp): the clock-in time
  * clock_out (timestamp): the clock-out time
- WorkShift:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the work shift
  * start_time (time): the start time of the shift
  * end_time (time): the end time of the shift
- AvailabilityException:
  * id (primary key, int): unique identifier for the availability exception
  * employee_id (foreign key, int): the employee this exception belongs to
  * start_date (date): the start date of the exception
  * end_date (date): the end date of the exception
  * type (varchar): the type of the exception (e.g., day off, medical leave, vacation, training)

STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES:
- A hospital has multiple departments (one-to-many).
- A department belongs to one hospital (many-to-one).
- A department has one coordinator (one-to-one).
- A personnel can be an employee, patient, or visitor (one-to-one).
- An employee can have multiple addresses (one-to-many).
- An employee can have one identification card (one-to-one).
- An employee can have multiple time clock records (one-to-many).
- An employee can have multiple work shifts (one-to-many).
- An employee can have multiple availability exceptions (one-to-many).
- A patient can have multiple hospital admissions (one-to-many).
- A visitor can have multiple visits (one-to-many).

RELATIONSHIPS:
- Hospital relates to Department (a hospital has multiple departments)
- Department relates to Hospital (a department belongs to one hospital)
- Department relates to Employee (a department has one coordinator)
- Personnel relates to Employee (a personnel can be an employee)
- Personnel relates to Patient (a personnel can be a patient)
- Personnel relates to Visitor (a personnel can be a visitor)
- Employee relates to Address (an employee can have multiple addresses)
- Employee relates to IdentificationCard (an employee can have one identification card)
- Employee relates to TimeClockRecord (an employee can have multiple time clock records)
- Employee relates to WorkShift (an employee can have multiple work shifts)
- Employee relates to AvailabilityException (an employee can have multiple availability exceptions)

STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS:
- Hospital to Department: 1--* (a hospital has multiple departments, a department belongs to one hospital)
  - Hospital: id (primary key)
  - Department: hospital_id (foreign key)
- Department to Employee (coordinator): 1--1 (a department has one coordinator, an employee can be a coordinator of one department)
  - Department: id (primary key)
  - Employee: id (primary key), department_id (foreign key)
- Personnel to Employee: 1--1 (a personnel can be an employee)
  - Personnel: id (primary key)
  - Employee: personnel_id (foreign key)
- Personnel to Patient: 1--1 (a personnel can be a patient)
  - Personnel: id (primary key)
  - Patient: personnel_id (foreign key)
- Personnel to Visitor: 1--1 (a personnel can be a visitor)
  - Personnel: id (primary key)
  - Visitor: personnel_id (foreign key)
- Employee to Address: 1--* (an employee can have multiple addresses)
  - Employee: id (primary key)
  - Address: employee_id (foreign key)
- Employee to IdentificationCard: 1--1 (an employee can have one identification card)
  - Employee: id (primary key)
  - IdentificationCard: employee_id (foreign key)
- Employee to TimeClockRecord: 1--* (an employee can have multiple time clock records)
  - Employee: id (primary key)
  - TimeClockRecord: employee_id (foreign key)
- Employee to WorkShift: 1--* (an employee can have multiple work shifts)
  - Employee: id (primary key)
  - WorkShift: id (primary key), employee_id (foreign key in EmployeeSchedule)
- Employee to AvailabilityException: 1--* (an employee can have multiple availability exceptions)
  - Employee: id (primary key)
  - AvailabilityException: employee_id (foreign key)

CARDINALITY & FK:
- Hospital: id --1--* hospital_id-- Department
- Department: id --1--1 department_id-- Employee
- Personnel: id --1--1 personnel_id-- Employee
- Personnel: id --1--1 personnel_id-- Patient
- Personnel: id --1--1 personnel_id-- Visitor
- Employee: id --1--* employee_id-- Address
- Employee: id --1--1 employee_id-- IdentificationCard
- Employee: id --1--* employee_id-- TimeClockRecord
- Employee: id --1--* employee_id-- WorkShift (through EmployeeSchedule)
- Employee: id --1--* employee_id-- AvailabilityException

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA:
- Each table has exactly one primary key.
- Each foreign key references an existing table and column.
- Cardinalities are logically consistent with the domain.
- Many-to-many relationships are handled with intermediary tables (e.g., EmployeeSchedule).
- No orphaned tables or attributes.

VALIDATION:
- All primary keys defined and unique
- All foreign keys valid
- Cardinalities are logical

formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital contains departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network must offer every service: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it provides (available teams and departments).
Departments in the network include:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1. Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader/responsible person for every shift (i.e., there is always someone accountable for the department at any time).
2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. A single person may assume more than one role (for example, a physician may be admitted as a patient outside working hours).
* Physicians may work at multiple units under different contract types (e.g., CLT: permanent employment, PJ: contracted as a legal entity, on-call/shift, daily rate).
* Residents are licensed physicians in training who attend to patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
* VIP patients have special rules for care and enhanced privacy (these rules relate to privacy and procedures, not to the medical quality of care).
* Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
* The hospital records the following for every person who accesses its facilities: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF = Brazilian taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
* Each patient receives an identification wristband.
* The system must record patient movements between departments, including admission, inter-department transfers and discharge.
* A complete history of the departments visited by each patient must be maintained.
* The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
* Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
* The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
* Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
* Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
* Not all patients are allowed to have an accompanying person. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant people; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when deemed necessary by a physician or social services.
* The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
* Access outside visiting hours must be blocked unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
* Employees must be clearly identified.
* A researcher can only be a nurse or a physician (i.e., researcher is not an independent occupational category separate from clinical roles).
* Still, any employee may be treated by the hospital, act as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4. Addresses
* A person may have more than one registered address.
* Employees periodically update their addresses.
* When possible, patients and visitors should also have their addresses updated.
* It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
* If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should issue an alert requesting a registration update.
2.5. Identification Cards
* Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
* Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
* When a card is in use it is marked active.
* Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
* Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
The hospital units require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or being assigned impractical schedules.
* Professionals follow different work routines.
* Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
* Different hospitals in the network may maintain different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
* It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duty, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1. Time & Attendance Records
* The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
* As noted, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
* Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
* Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
* Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
* Nurses may follow fixed schedules or rotate through shifts.
* Physicians may have variable schedules and may visit the hospital only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are complete.
* The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
* The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
* Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
* Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1. Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. Upon reporting a loss:
* the card is automatically invalidated;
* a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
* every attempted access with the lost card is blocked and logged;
* the event generates an alert for security.
A card history (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2. Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record:
   * the person involved;
   * reason for the failure;
   * the read attempt;
   * manual verification performed (security/reception staff responsible).
Manual entry/exit validation using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked as inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)
