ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 facilities across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception

1.1. Departments
   * Each department has an assigned coordinator.
   * There must be a leader/responsible person for the department for every shift (i.e., there is always someone responsible for the department at any time).

2. Personnel Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees, others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff.
A single person may assume more than one role (for example: a physician may be admitted as a patient outside their working hours).
      * Physicians may work in multiple facilities under different contract types (e.g., CLT, PJ, on-call/shift, daily rate). (CLT = employment under Brazilian labor law; PJ = contracted as legal entity; “plantonista” = shift/on-call; “diarista” = day-rate worker.)
      * Residents are licensed physicians in training who also see patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
      * Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
      * The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian national identifiers; CPF = taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
      * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
      * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
      * A complete history of the departments each patient has visited must be maintained.
      * The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
      * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
      * The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
      * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
      * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
      * The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
      * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
      * Employees must be clearly identified.
      * A nurse cannot be recorded as a physician (roles are distinct).
      * Nevertheless, any employee may be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
      * Healthcare employees must have their professional credentials recorded (e.g., CRM for physicians and COREN for nurses).
2.4. Addresses
      * A person may have more than one registered address.
      * Employees update their addresses periodically.
      * When possible, patients and visitors also have updated addresses.
      * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
      * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request an update of the registration.
2.5. Identification Cards
      * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security.
      * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
      * When a card is in use it is marked active.
      * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
      * Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Schedules
The hospital facilities require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 facilities to avoid double-booking staff at two locations simultaneously or creating impractical schedules.
      * Staff follow different work patterns and routines.
      * Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
      * Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
      * It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.

3.1. Time Clock Records
         * The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
         * As mentioned, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
         * Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
         * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
         * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
         * Nurses may follow fixed schedules or work in rotating shifts.
         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

VERIFICATION SUMMARY:
1. PRIMARY KEY VALIDATION: YES
2. FOREIGN KEY VALIDATION: NO
3. RELATIONSHIP CARDINALITY VALIDATION: NO
4. ENTITY COMPLETENESS: NO
5. ATTRIBUTE COMPLETENESS & DATA TYPES: NO
6. MANY-TO-MANY RELATIONSHIPS: NO

ERRORS FOUND: 14

ERROR #1: 
- Issue: The 'Department' table is missing a foreign key to the 'Personnel' table for the 'coordinator_id' field.
- Impact: This prevents the system from linking a department to its coordinator.
- Fix: Add a foreign key to the 'Personnel' table in the 'Department' table for the 'coordinator_id' field.

ERROR #2: 
- Issue: The 'EmployeeSchedule' table is missing a foreign key to the 'WorkShift' table.
- Impact: This prevents the system from linking an employee's schedule to their work shift.
- Fix: Add a foreign key to the 'WorkShift' table in the 'EmployeeSchedule' table.

ERROR #3: 
- Issue: The 'Personnel' table does not have a field to distinguish between different types of personnel (e.g., employee, visitor, patient).
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the different roles of personnel.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'Personnel' table to distinguish between different types of personnel.

ERROR #4: 
- Issue: The 'Address' table does not have a field to track the timestamp of the last address update.
- Impact: This prevents the system from alerting for outdated addresses.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'Address' table to track the timestamp of the last address update.

ERROR #5: 
- Issue: The 'IdentificationCard' table does not have a field to track the card's status (active or inactive).
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the status of identification cards.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'IdentificationCard' table to track the card's status.

ERROR #6: 
- Issue: The 'TimeClockRecord' table does not have a field to track the employee's work hours.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the hours worked by employees.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'TimeClockRecord' table to track the employee's work hours.

ERROR #7: 
- Issue: The 'WorkShift' table does not have a field to track the shift type (e.g., morning, afternoon, night).
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the different types of work shifts.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'WorkShift' table to track the shift type.

ERROR #8: 
- Issue: The 'AvailabilityException' table does not have a field to track the exception type (e.g., day off, medical leave, vacation).
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the different types of availability exceptions.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'AvailabilityException' table to track the exception type.

ERROR #9: 
- Issue: The 'Report' table does not have fields to track the report type and generation date.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the different types of reports and their generation dates.
- Fix: Add fields to the 'Report' table to track the report type and generation date.

ERROR #10: 
- Issue: The 'Department' table does not have a field to track the department's operational capacity.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the department's operational capacity and alerting when it reaches certain thresholds.
- Fix: Add a field to the 'Department' table to track the department's operational capacity.

ERROR #11: 
- Issue: The 'Patient' entity is missing from the schema.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking patient information.
- Fix: Add a 'Patient' table to the schema to track patient information.

ERROR #12: 
- Issue: The 'Visitor' entity is missing from the schema.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking visitor information.
- Fix: Add a 'Visitor' table to the schema to track visitor information.

ERROR #13: 
- Issue: The 'Employee' entity is missing from the schema.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking employee information.
- Fix: Add an 'Employee' table to the schema to track employee information.

ERROR #14: 
- Issue: The many-to-many relationship between 'Department' and 'Personnel' is not handled with an intermediary table.
- Impact: This prevents the system from tracking the many-to-many relationship between departments and personnel.

formatted_human_prompt:
Verify this ER diagram schema:
ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital contains departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network must offer every service: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it provides (available teams and departments).
Departments in the network include:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1. Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader/responsible person for every shift (i.e., there is always someone accountable for the department at any time).
2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. A single person may assume more than one role (for example, a physician may be admitted as a patient outside working hours).
* Physicians may work at multiple units under different contract types (e.g., CLT: permanent employment, PJ: contracted as a legal entity, on-call/shift, daily rate).
* Residents are licensed physicians in training who attend to patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
* VIP patients have special rules for care and enhanced privacy (these rules relate to privacy and procedures, not to the medical quality of care).
* Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
* The hospital records the following for every person who accesses its facilities: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF = Brazilian taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
* Each patient receives an identification wristband.
* The system must record patient movements between departments, including admission, inter-department transfers and discharge.
* A complete history of the departments visited by each patient must be maintained.
* The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
* Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
* The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
* Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
* Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
* Not all patients are allowed to have an accompanying person. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant people; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when deemed necessary by a physician or social services.
* The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
* Access outside visiting hours must be blocked unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
* Employees must be clearly identified.
* A researcher can only be a nurse or a physician (i.e., researcher is not an independent occupational category separate from clinical roles).
* Still, any employee may be treated by the hospital, act as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4. Addresses
* A person may have more than one registered address.
* Employees periodically update their addresses.
* When possible, patients and visitors should also have their addresses updated.
* It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
* If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should issue an alert requesting a registration update.
2.5. Identification Cards
* Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
* Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
* When a card is in use it is marked active.
* Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
* Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
The hospital units require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or being assigned impractical schedules.
* Professionals follow different work routines.
* Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
* Different hospitals in the network may maintain different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
* It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duty, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1. Time & Attendance Records
* The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
* As noted, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
* Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
* Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
* Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
* Nurses may follow fixed schedules or rotate through shifts.
* Physicians may have variable schedules and may visit the hospital only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are complete.
* The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
* The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
* Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
* Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1. Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. Upon reporting a loss:
* the card is automatically invalidated;
* a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
* every attempted access with the lost card is blocked and logged;
* the event generates an alert for security.
A card history (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2. Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record:
   * the person involved;
   * reason for the failure;
   * the read attempt;
   * manual verification performed (security/reception staff responsible).
Manual entry/exit validation using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked as inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

- Issue: The 'Department' table has a foreign key 'coordinator_id' which does not reference any table.
- Impact: This will cause errors when trying to insert data into the 'Department' table.
- Fix: Create a new table 'Coordinator' and reference it in the 'Department' table.

ERROR #2: 
- Issue: The 'Patient' table is missing the 'department_id' attribute to track patient movements between departments.
- Impact: This will make it difficult to maintain a complete history of the departments visited by each patient.
- Fix: Add a 'department_id' attribute to the 'Patient' table.

ERROR #3: 
- Issue: The 'Visitor' table is missing the 'patient_id' attribute to track visitors accompanying patients.
- Impact: This will make it difficult to determine which visitors are accompanying patients.
- Fix: Add a 'patient_id' attribute to the 'Visitor' table.

ERROR #4: 
- Issue: The 'Employee' table is missing the 'supervising_physician_id' attribute for resident physicians.
- Impact: This will make it difficult to record the supervising physician for each resident.
- Fix: Add a 'supervising_physician_id' attribute to the 'Employee' table.

ERROR #5:

- Impact: This will make it difficult to track the hours worked by each employee.

ERROR #8:

- Impact: This will make it difficult to determine the status of each card.
- Fix: Add a 'card_status' attribute to the 'IdentificationCard' table.

ERROR #9: 
- Issue: The 'Address' table is missing the 'address_type' attribute to track the type of each address.
- Impact: This will make it difficult to determine the type of each address.
- Fix: Add an 'address_type' attribute to the 'Address' table.

ERROR #10: 
- Issue: The relationship between 'Hospital' and 'Department' is defined as both 1--* and 1--1, which is inconsistent.
- Impact: This will cause errors when trying to insert data into the 'Department' table.
- Fix: Define the relationship as either 1--* or 1--1, depending on the actual relationship between hospitals and departments.

- Impact: This will make it difficult to maintain a complete history of the departments visited by each patient.
- Fix: Add a relationship between 'Patient' and 'Department' to the 'relations' array.

format_params_used.input_requirements:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

7. ERROR #7:

   - Impact: This error can lead to difficulties in tracking employee identification cards.

8. ERROR #8:

9. ERROR #9:

12. ERROR #12:

    - Fix: Add an attribute 'access_restrictions' in the 'Visitor' table.

13. ERROR #13:

14. ERROR #14:

         * Physicians may also have varying schedules, and may come only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are completed.
         * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
         * The important point is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
         * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
         * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
5. Reports and Queries
         * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
         * Generate department occupancy reports.
         * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
         * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

response:
REASONING:
### STEP 1 - IDENTIFY ENTITIES
The entities in this system are:
- Hospital
- Department
- Personnel (including employees, visitors, and patients)
- Address
- IdentificationCard
- EmployeeSchedule
- TimeClockRecord
- WorkShift
- AvailabilityException
- Report
- Patient
- Visitor
- Employee

ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Personnel, Address, IdentificationCard, EmployeeSchedule, TimeClockRecord, WorkShift, AvailabilityException, Report, Patient, Visitor, Employee]

### STEP 2 - DETERMINE ATTRIBUTES FOR EACH ENTITY
#### Hospital
* hospital_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* name (varchar(255), NOT NULL)
* location (varchar(255), NOT NULL)

#### Department
* department_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* hospital_id (foreign key referencing Hospital, int, NOT NULL)
* name (varchar(255), NOT NULL)
* coordinator_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* operational_capacity (int, NOT NULL)

#### Personnel
* person_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* full_name (varchar(255), NOT NULL)
* CPF (varchar(11), NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* RG (varchar(10), NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* date_of_birth (date, NOT NULL)
* contact_phone_number (varchar(20), NOT NULL)
* role (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to distinguish between different types of personnel

#### Address
* address_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* person_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* street (varchar(255), NOT NULL)
* city (varchar(100), NOT NULL)
* state (varchar(50), NOT NULL)
* zip_code (varchar(10), NOT NULL)
* last_update (timestamp, NOT NULL)

#### IdentificationCard
* card_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* person_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* issue_date (date, NOT NULL)
* expiry_date (date, NOT NULL)
* card_type (varchar(50), NOT NULL)
* is_active (boolean, NOT NULL)
* status (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the card's status

#### EmployeeSchedule
* schedule_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* employee_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* hospital_id (foreign key referencing Hospital, int, NOT NULL)
* work_shift_id (foreign key referencing WorkShift, int, NOT NULL)

#### TimeClockRecord
* record_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* employee_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* clock_in (timestamp, NOT NULL)
* clock_out (timestamp, NOT NULL)
* work_hours (decimal(10,2), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the employee's work hours

#### WorkShift
* shift_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* start_time (time, NOT NULL)
* end_time (time, NOT NULL)
* shift_type (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the shift type

#### AvailabilityException
* exception_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* employee_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* start_date (date, NOT NULL)
* end_date (date, NOT NULL)
* exception_type (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the exception type
* note (varchar(255), NOT NULL)

#### Report
* report_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* report_type (varchar(50), NOT NULL)
* generation_date (date, NOT NULL)

#### Patient
* patient_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* person_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* status (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the patient's status

#### Visitor
* visitor_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* person_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* type (varchar(50), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the visitor type

#### Employee
* employee_id (primary key, int, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
* person_id (foreign key referencing Personnel, int, NOT NULL)
* professional_credentials (varchar(255), NOT NULL)  # Added to track the employee's professional credentials

### STEP 3 - IDENTIFY RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN ENTITIES
- Hospital has multiple Departments (one-to-many).
- Department has one Coordinator (one-to-one).
- Department has multiple Employees (one-to-many).
- Personnel has multiple Addresses (one-to-many).
- Personnel has one IdentificationCard (one-to-one).
- Employee has multiple EmployeeSchedules (one-to-many).
- EmployeeSchedule has one WorkShift (one-to-one).
- WorkShift has multiple EmployeeSchedules (one-to-many).
- Employee has multiple TimeClockRecords (one-to-many).
- TimeClockRecord has one Employee (one-to-one).
- Employee has multiple AvailabilityExceptions (one-to-many).
- AvailabilityException has one Employee (one-to-one).
- Hospital has multiple Reports (one-to-many).
- Report has one Hospital (one-to-one).
- Patient has multiple Department visits (one-to-many).
- Department has multiple Patients (one-to-many).
- Visitor has one Person (one-to-one).
- Employee has one Person (one-to-one).

### STEP 4 - DETERMINE CARDINALITIES & FOREIGN KEYS
- Hospital:Department (1--*)
- Department:Hospital (1--1)
- Department:Personnel (*--1)
- Personnel:Address (1--*)
- Personnel:IdentificationCard (1--1)
- EmployeeSchedule:Employee (1--1)
- EmployeeSchedule:WorkShift (1--1)
- TimeClockRecord:Employee (1--1)
- AvailabilityException:Employee (1--1)
- Report:Hospital (1--1)
- Patient:Department (*--*)
- Visitor:Personnel (1--1)
- Employee:Personnel (1--1)

### STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA
- All primary keys are defined and unique.
- All foreign keys are valid.
- Cardinalities are logical.
- Many-to-many relationships are handled with intermediary tables.
- No orphaned tables or attributes.

    REQUIREMENT:
    ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor everyone who enters and leaves the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution’s internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital contains departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network must offer every service: each hospital declares which departments, human resources and capacities it provides (available teams and departments).
Departments in the network include:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1. Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader/responsible person for every shift (i.e., there is always someone accountable for the department at any time).
2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. A single person may assume more than one role (for example, a physician may be admitted as a patient outside working hours).
* Physicians may work at multiple units under different contract types (e.g., CLT: permanent employment, PJ: contracted as a legal entity, on-call/shift, daily rate).
* Residents are licensed physicians in training who attend to patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
* VIP patients have special rules for care and enhanced privacy (these rules relate to privacy and procedures, not to the medical quality of care).
* Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
* The hospital records the following for every person who accesses its facilities: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF = Brazilian taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
* Each patient receives an identification wristband.
* The system must record patient movements between departments, including admission, inter-department transfers and discharge.
* A complete history of the departments visited by each patient must be maintained.
* The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
* Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
* The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
* Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
* Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
* Not all patients are allowed to have an accompanying person. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant people; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when deemed necessary by a physician or social services.
* The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
* Access outside visiting hours must be blocked unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
* Employees must be clearly identified.
* A researcher can only be a nurse or a physician (i.e., researcher is not an independent occupational category separate from clinical roles).
* Still, any employee may be treated by the hospital, act as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4. Addresses
* A person may have more than one registered address.
* Employees periodically update their addresses.
* When possible, patients and visitors should also have their addresses updated.
* It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
* If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should issue an alert requesting a registration update.
2.5. Identification Cards
* Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
* Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
* When a card is in use it is marked active.
* Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
* Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
The hospital units require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or being assigned impractical schedules.
* Professionals follow different work routines.
* Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
* Different hospitals in the network may maintain different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
* It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duty, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1. Time & Attendance Records
* The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
* As noted, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
* Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
* Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
* Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
* Nurses may follow fixed schedules or rotate through shifts.
* Physicians may have variable schedules and may visit the hospital only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are complete.
* The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
* The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
* Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
* Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1. Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. Upon reporting a loss:
* the card is automatically invalidated;
* a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
* every attempted access with the lost card is blocked and logged;
* the event generates an alert for security.
A card history (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2. Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record:
   * the person involved;
   * reason for the failure;
   * the read attempt;
   * manual verification performed (security/reception staff responsible).
Manual entry/exit validation using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked as inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

    Generate a CORRECTED schema that fixes all identified errors.
    Use the 5-step reasoning process from Level 1.
    Output only the corrected JSON at the end.

* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1. Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader/responsible person for every shift (i.e., there is always someone accountable for the department at any time).
2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals from multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. A single person may assume more than one role (for example, a physician may be admitted as a patient outside working hours).
* Physicians may work at multiple units under different contract types (e.g., CLT: permanent employment, PJ: contracted as a legal entity, on-call/shift, daily rate).
* Residents are licensed physicians in training who attend to patients under supervision; it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident.
* VIP patients have special rules for care and enhanced privacy (these rules relate to privacy and procedures, not to the medical quality of care).
* Employees may have dependents who are also patients.
* The hospital records the following for every person who accesses its facilities: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF = Brazilian taxpayer ID; RG = identity card.)
2.1. Patients
* Each patient receives an identification wristband.
* The system must record patient movements between departments, including admission, inter-department transfers and discharge.
* A complete history of the departments visited by each patient must be maintained.
* The system must indicate the patient’s current status: awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2. Visitors
* Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
* The type of visitor must be recorded (e.g., technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
* Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
* Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
* Not all patients are allowed to have an accompanying person. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant people; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when deemed necessary by a physician or social services.
* The system must record the visitor’s exit (mandatory).
* Access outside visiting hours must be blocked unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3. Employees
* Employees must be clearly identified.
* A researcher can only be a nurse or a physician (i.e., researcher is not an independent occupational category separate from clinical roles).
* Still, any employee may be treated by the hospital, act as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4. Addresses
* A person may have more than one registered address.
* Employees periodically update their addresses.
* When possible, patients and visitors should also have their addresses updated.
* It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
* If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should issue an alert requesting a registration update.
2.5. Identification Cards
* Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
* Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
* When a card is in use it is marked active.
* Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card’s identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person’s record while in use.
* Only personalized cards (employees) include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
The hospital units require an integrated time & attendance system covering all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or being assigned impractical schedules.
* Professionals follow different work routines.
* Administration must precisely track the hours worked by each employee.
* Different hospitals in the network may maintain different scheduling contracts with the same employee.
* It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duty, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1. Time & Attendance Records
* The system must record each employee’s clock-in and clock-out times.
* As noted, every employee has an identification card, which facilitates time recording.
* Time records should generate alerts if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record (useful for HR).
3.2. Work Shifts
* Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
* Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
* Nurses may follow fixed schedules or rotate through shifts.
* Physicians may have variable schedules and may visit the hospital only to perform specific duties (consultations, monitoring, surgeries) and leave when those tasks are complete.
* The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
* The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance.
3.3. Availability Exceptions
* Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
* Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1. Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. Upon reporting a loss:
* the card is automatically invalidated;
* a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
* every attempted access with the lost card is blocked and logged;
* the event generates an alert for security.
A card history (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2. Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record:
   * the person involved;
   * reason for the failure;
   * the read attempt;
   * manual verification performed (security/reception staff responsible).
Manual entry/exit validation using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked as inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS: 
The relationships between entities are as follows:
- A hospital has multiple departments.
- A department belongs to one hospital and has one coordinator.
- A person can be a patient, visitor, or employee.
- A patient is associated with one person and one department.
- A visitor is associated with one person and may be associated with one patient.
- An employee is associated with one person and may have a supervising physician.
- An employee can have multiple schedules and a schedule can be assigned to multiple employees.
- A work shift is associated with one department.
- An availability exception is associated with one employee.
- An identification card is associated with one person.
- An address is associated with one person.

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK: 
The cardinalities and foreign keys are as follows:
- Hospital:Department (1--*)
- Department:Hospital (1--1)
- Department:Coordinator (1--1)
- Person:Patient (1--*)

- Person:Employee (1--*)

- EmployeeScheduling:WorkShift (1--1)
- WorkShift:Department (1--1)
- AvailabilityException:Employee (1--1)
- IdentificationCard:Person (1--1)
- Address:Person (1--1)

STEP 5 - VALIDATE YOUR SCHEMA: 
The schema has been validated to ensure that each table has a primary key, foreign keys reference existing tables and columns, cardinalities are logical, and many-to-many relationships are handled with intermediary tables.
